title: Intelligence Briefing Dashboard
subtitle: Weekly Conflict Monitor · All Regions
sources: Reuters · AP News · Al Jazeera · ACLED · US Embassy Alerts · LiveUAMap · BNO News · ISW · British Defence Intelligence · Kyiv Post · Sudan Tribune · Irrawaddy · CNN · NBC · CBS · Fox News · HRW · UN News · CFR · Times of Israel · NPR · CNBC · Atlantic Council · Sudan Tribune · US State Dept · OHCHR · Wikipedia · Washington Post

regions.name: Middle East
overview: Iran's succession crisis is now the single largest wildcard in the region: Mojtaba Khamenei was entirely absent from his own father's multi-day state funeral (3-6 July, continuing through burial on 9 July), fueling speculation of an incapacitated figurehead with the IRGC actually running the state, even as the US-Iran Doha track produced incremental progress (a frozen-asset unlock mechanism, a violations channel) before pausing for the funeral — the unresolved Hormuz transit-fee dispute remains a live trigger once talks resume around 11 July.

The ceasefire architecture across Lebanon and Gaza is eroding in parallel rather than collapsing outright: both show near-daily Israeli strikes and targeted killings continuing beneath a nominally intact truce, with a hardening political dimension — Hezbollah's rejection of the Lebanon framework and rights-group criticism of its Article 13, and Finance Minister Smotrich's explicit push for new Gaza settlements opposed by Washington.

Yemen produced the week's sharpest escalation: a Houthi-government clash at Jabal Dabbas (4-5 July) was described as the deadliest in years, compounded by a Houthi-Saudi war of words after an alleged Saudi attempt to block an Iranian evacuee flight into Sanaa and an unclaimed Red Sea shipping attack — reversing the prior 'standdown holding' assessment.

Syria's transitional government faces its most concentrated multi-front stress test yet: within a 72-hour span, Damascus suffered a lethal unclaimed IED attack near its own Palace of Justice, the Sweida Druze ceasefire ruptured again at Tal Hadid, and Israeli incursions/shelling continued in Daraa/Quneitra — even as Libya's parallel Haftar-GNU unification track advanced diplomatically alongside a death-in-custody scandal exposing its own fragility.

countries.name: Gaza
incidents: 29 Jun — Khan Younis (al-Mawasi): Israeli strike hit a displacement tent after a brief evacuation warning, killing a mother and her 1-year-old daughter; same-day strikes across southern/central Gaza killed at least 8 people including 2 children.

1 Jul — Gaza City (al-Hilu station): Israeli drone strike killed at least 3 Palestinians; IDF separately claimed 4 Hamas fighters killed without providing evidence, as the UN warned Israeli-controlled 'access-restricted' zones now cover roughly 65% of Gaza's land.

2 Jul — Gaza Strip: The 1,000-day mark since 7 October 2023 passed with a cumulative toll cited at 73,066+ killed and more than 1,000 killed since the October ceasefire began; roughly 90% of structures destroyed.

3-5 Jul — Gaza Strip: IDF/Shin Bet announced a strike killing a named Hamas Shuja'iyya Battalion security official; Finance Minister Smotrich said Israel is 'ready to build three settlements in Gaza immediately' pending Netanyahu's approval, a significant policy escalation opposed by Washington.
countries.coa: The ceasefire remains formally intact but is being hollowed out from within — near-daily strikes, expanding Israeli-controlled 'access-restricted' zones now covering roughly 65% of the Strip, and Smotrich's explicit push for new settlements signal a shift from tactical enforcement toward permanent territorial entrenchment. CRITICAL maintained; the settlement push is the clearest 90-day indicator that a negotiated reconstruction pathway is being foreclosed rather than pursued.

countries.name: Lebanon
incidents: 30 Jun — Southern Lebanon: Netanyahu, visiting troops, said Israel 'will not leave southern Lebanon until the threat has disappeared'; Lebanese health ministry reported roughly 25 killed in Israeli strikes over the prior 24 hours, pushing the cumulative toll since 2 March above 3,600.

2-3 Jul — Nabatieh al-Fawqa, Braashit, Kounine, Baraachit, Bint Jbeil, Beit Yahoun: Israeli strikes and home demolitions hit roughly 10 Hezbollah sites and a weapons-transfer truck after a Hezbollah gunman wounded an Israeli reservist; President Aoun publicly defended the 26 June trilateral framework against NGO criticism that it 'betrays war crimes victims.'
4 Jul — Majdal Zoun: IDF killed an armed Hezbollah operative after a manhunt inside the declared buffer zone.
5 Jul — Ali Taher ridge: Israeli strike and an Egoz-unit raid killed a Hezbollah operative emerging from a tunnel shaft believed to be part of a roughly 30-operative tunnel 'nerve center.'
countries.coa: Israeli strikes, demolitions, and targeted killings of Hezbollah operatives continue at an unchanged near-daily tempo — this is attritional continuation rather than either collapse into full war or genuine de-escalation. The political fight over the 26 June framework is now as significant as the kinetics. CRITICAL maintained; renewed full-scale war remains the base case absent a diplomatic breakthrough.

countries.name: Iran
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Doha: US and Iranian negotiators met directly under the 60-day MoU window; Iran agreed to open a violations/compliance communication channel and talks concluded 2 July with agreement to unlock roughly $6-12 billion in frozen Iranian assets held in Qatar, though the Hormuz transit-toll dispute remains unresolved.

3-4 Jul — Tehran (Grand Mosalla): State funeral proceeds for Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei with millions of mourners; new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei did not appear at his own father's funeral, with three brothers attending in his place, fueling speculation he remains incapacitated from wounds sustained in the February strikes.
4 Jul — Tehran: Iran's ambassador to China confirmed Iran will 'definitely' begin charging Hormuz transit fees, a core unresolved dispute with Washington.

5-6 Jul — Tehran/Qom/Najaf/Mashhad: Mourning and funeral procession continue, with talks paused until roughly 11 July; the IRGC publicly warned any US 'miscalculation' would draw a response 'more crushing than ever.'
countries.coa: The shooting war with the US and Israel remains paused, not resolved, and Iran's succession crisis has deepened rather than stabilized — Mojtaba Khamenei's total public absence from his father's funeral suggests a possibly incapacitated figurehead with the IRGC effectively running the state. CRITICAL maintained; succession consolidation and the unresolved Hormuz dispute are the two indicators to watch in the 90-day window.

countries.name: Yemen
incidents: 3 Jul — Sanaa: Houthi forces claimed they confronted Saudi warplanes attempting to block an Iranian evacuee flight — the first Tehran-Sanaa flight in roughly a decade — landing at Sanaa airport; Houthi spokesman Yahya Saree threatened a 'comprehensive response targeting [Saudi] airports and vital interests' if repeated.
4 Jul — Riyadh: The Saudi-led coalition spokesman pledged to respond with 'unprecedented determination and force' to any attack on the Kingdom.

4-5 Jul — Jabal Dabbas, Hodeidah governorate: Houthi forces briefly overran government positions before being repelled in what a government officer called the deadliest Houthi attack in years; at least 16 government troops killed and 22 wounded, with the government claiming 50+ Houthi fighters killed.
5 Jul — Off Hodeidah: An unidentified skiff attacked a bulk carrier roughly 30 nautical miles southwest of Hodeidah with its AIS disabled; no group claimed responsibility.
countries.coa: Upgraded to CRITICAL. What had been assessed as a holding Red Sea standdown reversed sharply this week — the deadliest Houthi-government ground clash in years, an explicit Houthi threat against Saudi airports, a Saudi vow of 'unprecedented force,' and an unclaimed shipping attack all landed within 48 hours. This risks reconnecting Yemen to the broader Iran-Israel-US conflict via Saudi Arabia; close monitoring of both the Hodeidah front and Red Sea shipping is warranted.

countries.name: Israel
incidents: 3 Jul — Israel: An American citizen was charged with espionage for Iran via cryptocurrency payments, reflecting continued internal counter-espionage activity tied to the war.

5 Jul — Mount Hermon buffer zone: The IDF detained roughly 100 Israeli settler activists who breached the buffer zone onto the Syrian side of Mount Hermon attempting to establish a settlement, calling it a 'serious incident and criminal offense.'
Ongoing — Israel confirmed this week it deployed Iron Dome batteries to the UAE during the war, a disclosure reflecting the conflict's regional air-defense footprint.
countries.coa: No new strikes landed on Israeli territory this week, but Israel remains simultaneously engaged in active operations across Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza, and the settler buffer-zone incursion into Syria adds a new friction point with the potential to draw a direct Israeli-Syrian confrontation. HIGH maintained; the Syrian buffer-zone settler movement is a new indicator to watch.

countries.name: Iraq
incidents: Ongoing — Baghdad: PM al-Zaidi's anti-corruption campaign has detained at least 67 people, backed publicly by the Sadrist 'Shia National Movement.'

Ongoing — Nationwide: The government's 30 September deadline for Iran-backed militias to disarm remains the central story; Asaib Ahl al-Haq and Kataib Imam Ali have agreed to disarm, while Kataib Hezbollah and Kataib Sayyid al-Shuhada have publicly refused, citing continued 'occupation.'
countries.coa: No confirmed militia attacks (rocket, drone, IED) this week, consistent with the operational pause noted previously. MODERATE maintained; the refusal of Kataib Hezbollah and Kataib Sayyid al-Shuhada to disarm ahead of the 30 September deadline is a latent flashpoint rather than a resolved one, and force-protection posture at US facilities should be maintained.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
incidents: 2 Jul — Riyadh: Saudi Arabia signed a security-cooperation MOU with Pakistan.
3 Jul — New York: Saudi Arabia participated in UN Counter-Terrorism Week.

3-4 Jul — Sanaa/Riyadh: Houthis accused Saudi Arabia of an airspace intrusion near Sanaa; Riyadh responded with a public vow of 'unprecedented force' against any attack on the Kingdom, though no strike landed on Saudi soil.
countries.coa: No attacks landed on Saudi territory this week and Riyadh continues active diplomatic and security normalization, but the sharp verbal exchange with the Houthis is a new risk vector — if acted on, it would reverse the de-escalation trend quickly. MODERATE maintained; Hormuz shipping remains reopened but reduced (roughly 25-35 transits/day versus about 110/day pre-war) ahead of Iran's planned transit-fee imposition around 17 August.

countries.name: Syria
incidents: 2 Jul — Damascus (al-Hijaz, near the Palace of Justice): An IED hidden under a café table on al-Nasr Street killed 10 people, including six lawyers, and wounded 22; no group has claimed responsibility.

2-3 Jul — Tal Hadid, western Suwayda province: Renewed heavy clashes between Druze National Guard and government Internal Security Forces using heavy weapons and mortars hit residential areas, rupturing a roughly year-old local ceasefire; tolls disputed by both sides.

2 Jul — Daraa/Quneitra (Yarmouk Basin): Israeli artillery shelling hit Abdeen/Jamlah and farmland near Bariqa/Kodana, part of roughly 300 Israeli operations logged in Daraa/Quneitra in June alone; Syria's foreign ministry condemned it as a sovereignty violation.
4 Jul — Nationwide: SOHR reported 438 civilians killed nationwide since the start of 2026.
countries.coa: Three concurrent stress points converged within 72 hours — the Damascus bombing near the seat of government, renewed Sweida/Druze fighting, and continued Israeli incursions in the south — suggesting the transitional government's authority is weakening this week rather than stabilizing, even as it tries to project normalization through a new parliament. HIGH maintained; Druze-government dialogue, accountability for the Damascus bombing, and the Israeli buffer-zone settler incident are the near-term indicators to watch.

countries.name: Egypt
incidents: Ongoing — Cairo: Egypt marked the anniversary of the 30 June Revolution; no dated security incident was identified inside Egypt this week.
2 Jul — Tripoli: Egypt announced resumption of consular services in Libya's capital from 12 July, ending a decade-long halt.
countries.coa: A genuinely quiet week for new domestic security incidents. MODERATE maintained; Egypt continues its role as an active Gaza ceasefire guarantor alongside Qatar and Turkey, and the Libya consular reopening reflects a broader regional normalization push worth tracking.

countries.name: Jordan
incidents: Ongoing — Amman: No new dated security incident was identified this week; the most recent notable event (Jordanian Armed Forces intercepting an Iranian missile aimed at Azraq) remains from 11 June, three weeks prior to this window.
countries.coa: A genuinely quiet week in available reporting. MODERATE maintained; latent risk from Gaza/Lebanon-linked unrest potential and Syria-border smuggling networks remains unrealized but unaddressed.

countries.name: Libya
incidents: 29 Jun — Washington: Lt. Gen. Saddam Haftar met US Secretary of State Rubio as part of a US-pushed power-sharing formula centered on a new Presidential Council; Libya's election roadmap now targets 17 February 2027.
30 Jun — Sorman: Activist Abdelmoneim al-Mureimi was abducted by Tripoli's Internal Security Agency.

3-4 Jul — Tripoli: Al-Mureimi died in state custody under disputed circumstances (official account: fell during interrogation; family disputes this), sparking protests; UNSMIL called the arrest a 'kidnapping' and demanded a transparent investigation.
2 Jul — Tripoli: Egypt announced resumption of consular services in Libya from 12 July after a decade-long halt, a further normalization signal.
countries.coa: The US-brokered Haftar-GNU unification track is genuinely advancing diplomatically, but a high-profile death in custody and a UNSMIL rebuke this week expose the fragility of the security apparatus underpinning any deal — analysts continue to flag real risk the elite bargain unravels from excluding militias, tribes, and civil society. MODERATE maintained.

countries.name: Tunisia
incidents: Ongoing — Gabès: The anti-pollution protest movement remains a live grievance with no new dated incident this week.
Ongoing — Tunis: The civil-society crackdown continues as an unresolved legal process, not a new incident.
countries.coa: A genuinely quiet week for new security incidents. MODERATE maintained; the Gabès protest movement and civil-society crackdown remain the primary domestic risk factors to monitor.

regions.name: Ukraine/Russia
overview: Ukraine's deadliest Kyiv strike in years (31 killed, 1-2 July) coincided with Ukraine's continued deep-strike drone campaign hitting Russian refineries (Kstovo struck twice in ten days, Ufa hit again), driving an openly acknowledged, worsening Russian fuel crisis, even as the ground front largely stalemates apart from a disputed Russian claim to have captured Kostiantynivka, the southernmost anchor of Ukraine's Donbas fortress belt.

Moldova's government collapsed 3 July — PM Alexandru Munteanu resigned amid a corruption scandal — inside an active Russian hybrid-interference campaign, with the pro-Russian Party of Socialists already refusing to consult with President Sandu on a successor, positioning to obstruct the government-formation process.

The Belarus-Ukraine drone-relay standoff resolved in Kyiv's favor this week, with Russian guidance-relay equipment on Belarusian border towers going dark after Zelensky's ultimatum and large Shahed raids along the border ceasing, even as Lukashenko continues consulting directly with Moscow and diversifying diplomatically abroad.

The South Caucasus continues diverging along a pro-Western/pro-Russian axis: Armenia and Azerbaijan are consolidating a Western-oriented trajectory (von der Leyen's dual Baku-Yerevan visit and a EUR 200 million-plus connectivity package), while Georgia remains a static, entrenched authoritarian outlier, with Russian-aligned proxies using the Geneva talks to frame Georgia's Western tilt as a security threat.

countries.name: Ukraine
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Kyiv (nationwide, 30+ locations): Russia launched roughly 70-74 missiles (nearly half ballistic) and 496-500 attack drones on Kyiv and four other regions — the deadliest strike on the capital in years, killing 31 and injuring 102+; Poland scrambled jets and Finland restricted airspace.

3 Jul — Kostiantynivka, Donetsk Oblast: Russia's Ministry of Defense claimed capture of the city after a roughly 9-month battle; Zelensky and Ukraine's General Staff deny it and say fighting continues at the southernmost anchor of Ukraine's Donbas 'fortress belt.'

4-5 Jul — Occupied Crimea: Ukraine struck Hvardiiske airfield and hit 16 power stations across the peninsula in 48 hours, causing widespread blackouts, alongside strikes on logistics bridges and ammunition depots in Donetsk, Luhansk, and Kherson.
4 Jul — St. Petersburg area: Ukraine struck oil and military facilities near the city, extending the reach of its deep-strike campaign further into Russia.
countries.coa: Ukraine's deep-strike campaign continues escalating in tandem with Russia's own long-range terror bombing — the 1-2 July Kyiv strike is the deadliest on the capital in years, while the disputed fall of Kostiantynivka marks the first significant contested breach of Ukraine's fortress belt in months. CRITICAL maintained; no ceasefire progress, with the military track continuing but the political track still stuck on Donbas and security-guarantee issues.

countries.name: Russia
incidents: 1 Jul — Ufa, Bashkortostan: Ukrainian drones struck the refinery roughly 1,300km from the front for a second time in a week.

2 Jul — Kstovo, Nizhny Novgorod Oblast: The Lukoil-Nizhegorodnefteorgsintez refinery — Russia's 4th-largest and 2nd-largest gasoline producer — suspended operations after another drone strike, the same plant hit twice in ten days.
2-3 Jul — Belgorod/Bryansk Oblasts: Ukrainian strikes hit an industrial facility in Belgorod and killed a man in a Bryansk village; a passenger bus was struck in Bryansk region, injuring two drivers.

2-3 Jul — Nationwide: Putin publicly acknowledged a 'certain shortage' of fuel for the first time, calling it 'not critical' and rejecting ceasefire calls; gasoline reserves are down roughly 4% year-on-year, two-thirds of Russian regions report fuel issues, and Russia has begun importing fuel from Kazakhstan and India.
countries.coa: The refinery campaign has produced an acknowledged, worsening, nationwide fuel crisis with direct presidential admission, import dependency, and rationing — a genuine internal economic stressor even as Moscow shows no sign of policy change and retaliates with escalating strikes on Ukrainian population centers. HIGH maintained; watch for increased pressure on Belarus for direct military contribution as internal strain grows.

countries.name: Belarus
incidents: 22 Jun (resolved into this week) — Belarus-Ukraine border: Russian drone-guidance relay equipment on Belarusian border towers went dark after Zelensky's ultimatum, and large Shahed raids along the border have since ceased; Lukashenko met Putin 26 June to discuss the matter, which the Kremlin called 'aggressive.'
1 Jul — Jakarta: Lukashenko signed a Belarus-Indonesia cooperation roadmap for 2026-2030 during a foreign trip, continuing efforts to diversify diplomatically beyond Russia.
1 Jul — Minsk: Lukashenko pardoned 32 prisoners (28 for 'extremism' charges) on Independence Day; Viasna reports 800+ political prisoners remain jailed.
countries.coa: A rare instance of Ukrainian coercive diplomacy succeeding against Minsk on the drone-relay issue, but underlying risk is unchanged — Belarus remains a staging and launch platform for Russian strikes, Lukashenko is still consulting directly with Putin, and roughly 600 vehicles remain queued at Belarus-Poland/Lithuania crossings amid extended border controls. MODERATE maintained.

countries.name: Moldova
incidents: 3 Jul — Chișinău: PM Alexandru Munteanu resigned after 8 months in office amid the MoldATSA corruption scandal, triggering full government collapse; he cited being unable to govern 'in accordance with my principles and convictions.'

3-4 Jul — Chișinău: President Sandu announced consultations with parliamentary factions to nominate a new PM, who will then have 15 days to win a confidence vote; the Russian 'Russian House' cultural/influence center shut down and removed its flag from its building.

6 Jul — Chișinău: The pro-Russian Party of Socialists announced it refuses to consult with President Sandu on the new PM nomination, signaling likely parliamentary obstruction during government formation.

Ongoing — Transnistria: Leader Vadim Krasnoselsky continues calling the situation 'explosive,' rhetoric amplified by Russian state media, as the EU's Kallas warns Moldova faces continued Russian hybrid interference ('cash, content, coercion').
countries.coa: The government collapse creates a genuine vulnerability window that Russian hybrid/influence operations are already moving to exploit, with the pro-Russian Party of Socialists now openly positioning to obstruct the succession process. HIGH maintained; the outcome of the PM nomination over the next 1-2 weeks is the key indicator, and Western partners should expedite support for a stable transition.

countries.name: Georgia
incidents: 1 Jul — Geneva: The 67th round of the Geneva International Discussions on South Caucasus security concluded, with Abkhaz, South Ossetian, and Russian representatives voicing concern over Georgia's 'accelerating NATO integration.'

Ongoing — Tbilisi: Nightly protests (~500 people) continue near parliament; 150+ opposition figures remain imprisoned, and the OSCE's Moscow Mechanism report (invoked by 23 states) remains unresolved, dismissed by Georgian Dream as 'complete nonsense.'
countries.coa: No new acute incident this week beyond the routine Geneva talks, consistent with a pattern of grinding, sustained rather than escalating authoritarian consolidation. HIGH maintained; Western governments should hold firm on Ivanishvili sanctions and political-prisoner releases as preconditions for any partnership relaunch.

countries.name: Armenia
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Yerevan: European Commission President von der Leyen visited as part of her South Caucasus tour, discussing the peace process, connectivity, and reaffirming EU support.

Ongoing — Yerevan: Pashinyan's Civil Contract party continues consolidating its 7 June election win, though it fell short of the two-thirds majority needed for the constitutional referendum required by the Azerbaijan peace deal; formal CSTO withdrawal remains unfinalized.
countries.coa: No new security incident this week; the main story is continued Western institutional engagement reinforcing Armenia's post-election Western pivot. MODERATE maintained; watch for further Russian economic coercion and progress toward the constitutional referendum needed to finalize the Azerbaijan peace deal.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Baku: Von der Leyen's first Baku visit since 2022 produced a EUR 200 million Global Gateway connectivity package for the South Caucasus plus a dedicated EUR 20 million peace-fostering programme for border communities, alongside energy and transport-corridor discussions.

Ongoing — Border: The Armenia border remains formally closed; the Armenia-Azerbaijan peace agreement (initialed August 2025) remains unsigned pending Armenia's constitutional referendum removing territorial claims.
countries.coa: No clashes or military incidents this week — sustained EU engagement continues to anchor Baku to the Western-mediated peace track. LOW maintained; the stalled referendum requirement remains the principal slow-moving political risk rather than an acute security one.

regions.name: Sahel
overview: Militant coordination is deepening in Mali — a 4 July JNIM-Azawad Liberation Front joint multi-front offensive across northern, central, and southern Mali simultaneously — even as it fragments into competition in Niger between JNIM and IS-Sahel, while state security architecture shows new cracks: a reported Niger military mutiny over unresourced Tillabéri deployments, independent of any single battlefield loss.

Sudan's El Obeid has drawn an unusual density of top-level international alarm this week — a 29-country coalition statement, a UN Human Rights Council urgent debate, and an HRW atrocity-prevention warning — without the city having yet fallen; RSF now controls all routes into the city except the eastern approach, and the gap between rhetoric and protective action is the key indicator to watch.

Nigeria's Borno abduction crisis remains unresolved, with dozens of students still held after the 29 June Lassa school attack — Nigeria's third mass school abduction since May 2026 — even as a partial rescue operation freed several captives near Chibok.

Information darkness continues to function as its own strategic signal: Burkina Faso and Chad again produced almost no independently verifiable incident reporting this week despite presumed-ongoing sieges and unresolved insurgencies, a monitoring gap rather than evidence of calm.

countries.name: Mali
incidents: 4 Jul — Aguelhok, Anefis, Gao, Sévaré, Kéniéroba, Konna, Somadougou: JNIM and the Azawad Liberation Front (FLA) carried out a coordinated multi-front offensive against army and pro-government positions across northern, central, and southern Mali simultaneously — the first joint JNIM-FLA offensive since the late-April attacks that killed Mali's defense minister; the army says it repelled the attacks with Africa Corps support, claiming 20 militants killed in Sévaré and 6 in Gao.

Ongoing — Bamako approaches: JNIM's fuel blockade (300+ tankers destroyed since September 2025) continues to strangle the capital; a 796-tanker convoy arrived under heavy military escort in late June, but the supply corridor remains an active kill zone.
countries.coa: The 4 July multi-front coordinated offensive is the most significant escalation since April, demonstrating operational fusion between JNIM and the FLA and the junta's inability to secure even central garrison towns like Sévaré, let alone the Bamako supply corridor. CRITICAL maintained; this is now sustained siege warfare compounded by an expanding two-front insurgency.

countries.name: Niger
incidents: 3-4 Jul — Niamey: Unconfirmed but multiply-sourced reports describe a limited mutiny by an armored unit of the Niger Armed Forces, reportedly refusing deployment to Tillabéri frontline positions over inadequate equipment; the Presidential Guard reinforced the presidential palace with armored vehicles overnight.

Ongoing — Tillabéri region: Continued JNIM and IS-Sahel activity in the deadliest area of the central Sahel for civilians, compounding strain on frontline units following the twice-attacked Niamey airport (most recently 18 June, 13 killed).
countries.coa: Military cohesion showing signs of strain under sustained deployment pressure is a new and qualitatively different risk layered on top of the existing insurgency — reported unit-level refusals to deploy to Tillabéri, if confirmed, would mark a genuine governance vulnerability for the junta. HIGH maintained pending confirmation of the mutiny reports; capital-reach capability by JNIM remains demonstrated from the June airport attack.

countries.name: Burkina Faso
incidents: Ongoing — Dori: Siege conditions consistent with prior MSF hospital-capacity collapse and district-operational suspension continue; no independently verified new dated incident was found this week, consistent with continued internet blackouts and journalist detentions suppressing outward news flow.

Early Jul — Nationwide: The junta passed a law abolishing the Independent National Electoral Commission and folding electoral oversight into the Interior Ministry, a further consolidation of junta control following the April dissolution of 118 NGOs.
countries.coa: The continued near-total absence of fresh sourced reporting is very likely an information blackout effect rather than genuine de-escalation. CRITICAL maintained on the sustained structural crisis; the electoral-oversight takeover this week signals deepening authoritarian consolidation alongside the unresolved security siege.

countries.name: Sudan
incidents: 2 Jul — El Obeid: RSF military movements were detected roughly 60km out in multiple directions from the city; 16 civilian/service targets have been struck to date, including a school where 8 students were injured by drone shrapnel during class, as food prices have risen 300% since the siege began.

3 Jul — Geneva: UN Human Rights Chief Volker Türk reported 15 drone strikes in a three-week period in June killed at least 45 civilians in El Obeid, striking markets, schools, fuel stations, and water infrastructure — the highest-intensity month of drone warfare of the war; a 29-country coalition led by Norway issued a joint warning at an urgent UN Human Rights Council debate.
Ongoing — El Obeid: RSF now controls all routes into the city except the eastern approach; a coalition of 46 aid organizations warned of 'imminent catastrophe.'
countries.coa: El Obeid has not yet fallen, but encirclement has tightened materially and the explicit 'El Fasher repeat' framing is now the dominant international narrative, backed by formal HRW atrocity-prevention findings and a 29-country diplomatic warning — a marked escalation in alarm even without a territorial change. CRITICAL maintained; the gap between rhetorical warnings and actual protective action (no ceasefire, no safe corridor) is the key thing to watch in the coming weeks.

countries.name: Chad
incidents: Ongoing — Lake Chad basin: No new confirmed attack on Chadian territory this week; the state of emergency declared 8 May following the Barka Tolorom base attacks (23-26 soldiers killed, including two generals) remains in force, with the underlying ISWAP-Boko Haram turf war over islands and fishing-community extortion (an estimated $41.5 million/year to ISWAP) continuing to drive instability.
countries.coa: No new confirmed incident this week; Chad remains at HIGH reflecting the still-unresolved Boko Haram/ISWAP contest for Lake Chad basin territory. Continued multinational coordination against both factions is warranted, alongside investment in early-warning reporting given the current information gap.

countries.name: Mauritania
incidents: Ongoing — Mali border: Structural friction continues over FAMA/Africa Corps operations in the Yélimané area accused of killing Mauritanian civilians and Fulani community members since February; Mauritania has called protection of its citizens a 'red line.' No new dated incident inside Mauritania itself was found this week.
countries.coa: Direct domestic incidents remain rare; Mauritania continues to stave off cross-border spillover from Mali's deepening crisis, though the Yélimané-area friction with Mali's junta forces remains a live diplomatic irritant. MODERATE maintained.

countries.name: Nigeria
incidents: 29 Jun — Lassa, Askira/Uba LGA, Borno State: Suspected Boko Haram/ISWAP gunmen stormed a Government Day Secondary School during NECO exams, killing 2 teachers and 1 student and abducting roughly 36-37 students and staff — Nigeria's third mass school abduction since May 2026; 8-10 were rescued in the initial response.

5 Jul — Talakawa, Chibok LGA, Borno State: Nigerian troops, acting on intelligence, rescued 6 additional abductees (4 adults, 2 infants) in an air-ground operation; reunification arrangements are underway.

1 Jul — Guzamala LGA, Borno State: ISWAP attacked a Forward Operating Base at a resettled community; Operation HADIN KAI troops repelled the attack, killing scores of insurgents, at a cost of 2 soldiers and 1 CJTF member.

Ongoing — Benue State: No new dated mass-casualty herder-farmer incident was confirmed this week, though the underlying retaliatory-violence cycle in the Middle Belt remains unresolved and frequently cited in current commentary.
countries.coa: The Borno abduction remains unresolved with dozens of children still held despite a partial rescue operation, and this is Nigeria's third mass school abduction since May with no sign of deterrence. CRITICAL maintained; sustained operational pressure, rescue negotiations, and hardening of school security in Borno are all required.

regions.name: Asia-Pacific
overview: The Pakistan-Afghanistan war has entered a self-reinforcing tit-for-tat cycle with no ceasefire: the 27 June Karachi Rangers HQ attack triggered Pakistani cross-border strikes into Afghanistan, which triggered Taliban drone/airstrike retaliation into Balochistan and KP on 1 July, now confirmed as an entrenched pattern rather than an isolated event.

China is running a simultaneous four-front grey-zone pressure campaign — economic coercion against Japan (40 entities under new export controls), a joint bomber patrol with Russia into South Korea's ADIZ, sustained carrier transits and patrols around Taiwan, and a direct naval standoff with the Philippines at Scarborough Shoal timed to the conclusion of a major US-allied exercise.

Myanmar's Arakan Army is close to full control of Rakhine State, with Kyaukphyu the decisive open question — China is shifting from passive investor to active on-the-ground security presence there, raising the risk of a first-ever direct China-AA confrontation at a Belt and Road flagship site.

North Korea's DMZ fortification is proceeding faster than South Korean intelligence projected and is now compounded by confirmed Russian technical assistance improving short-range missile precision — a capability escalation more significant this week than any single new test, alongside repeated defector/crossing incidents and heightened South Korean readiness.

countries.name: North Korea
incidents: 21-24 Jun (context, still driving this week's assessment) — DMZ: North Korea advanced barbed-wire fencing to within 82 meters of the Military Demarcation Line, versus a traditional 300-1,000m buffer, and cleared terrain to seed minefields 5-10m from the MDL; Seoul's Defense Ministry called it an armistice violation.

24 Jun-early Jul — DMZ: Two separate crossing incidents occurred — an unarmed KPA soldier walked south expressing intent to defect (the 4th known defection since President Lee took office), and a second incident involved armed KPA soldiers briefly crossing the MDL in pursuit of a defector.
24 Jun — Yeonpyeong Island: President Lee Jae Myung ordered Marine units to maintain readiness for an 'immediate and overwhelming' response to any provocation.
20 Jun (context) — Ukrainian intelligence reported Russian technology transfers have sharply improved North Korean KN-23/KN-24 ballistic missile accuracy, cutting the error margin from 1-3km to 1-5m.
countries.coa: No new missile test was confirmed inside this specific week, but DMZ militarization is accelerating faster than South Korean planners expected, and Russian-assisted precision upgrades to short-range systems represent a substantive capability jump. CRITICAL maintained; enhanced ISR coverage of DMZ activity and continued joint ROK-US monitoring of test cadence are warranted.

countries.name: Myanmar
incidents: Ongoing this week — Ayeyarwady Region: Fighting escalated across Lemyethna, Yegyi, and Thabaung townships as the Arakan Army (AA) and allies push from Rakhine Yoma toward the Gwa-Ngathaingchaung Road; junta forces are retreating, with reinforcements dispatched from Pathein-based Western Command and thousands of new civilian displacements.

Ongoing — Kyaukphyu SEZ: Heavy fighting continues around the China-backed deep-sea port; the junta passed a Private Security Services Law opening the door to Chinese security personnel deploying to protect CITIC's investment, and residents report Chinese security personnel have already begun arriving.

Ongoing — Rakhine State: The AA now holds 14 of 17 townships plus Paletwa in Chin State; only Sittwe, part of Kyaukphyu, and Manaung remain outside full AA control, with AA leadership reiterating a goal of full Rakhine control by end of 2027.
countries.coa: The Kyaukphyu situation is the key escalation risk to watch — Chinese security forces reportedly arriving on the ground raise the odds of a direct China-AA friction incident, a first for the Myanmar civil war. CRITICAL maintained; junta control in Rakhine is nearly collapsed and fighting is metastasizing into Ayeyarwady Region.

countries.name: Pakistan
incidents: 27 Jun — Karachi (Gulistan-i-Jauhar): Militants rammed a vehicle into a Sindh Rangers HQ gate and opened fire; 3 Rangers personnel and 3 attackers were killed. Jamaat-ul-Ahrar claimed responsibility, and a captured assailant was identified as an Afghan national.

29 Jun — Paktia, Paktika, Kunar provinces (Afghanistan) / Bajaur, KP: Pakistan launched retaliatory cross-border strikes, claiming 25 fighters killed, alongside a ground operation in Bajaur that killed several Jamaat-ul-Ahrar members including a senior commander.

1 Jul — Saranan, Balochistan / Khyber Pakhtunkhwa: Afghan Taliban forces fired drones into Pakistani territory near Saranan, injuring two people near a government school, and separately claimed airstrikes on alleged ISIS-K hideouts in Balochistan and KP; Pakistan says it intercepted or shot down 4 drones.

Ongoing — Durand Line: The Taliban claims Pakistani airstrikes have killed 36 civilians in eastern Afghanistan in the current escalation cycle, unverified independently; a prior Chinese-mediated ceasefire collapsed in June after roughly two months.
countries.coa: This week confirms a tightening tit-for-tat cycle with no ceasefire in effect — the Karachi Rangers attack triggered Pakistani cross-border strikes, which triggered Taliban drone/airstrike retaliation, now an entrenched pattern rather than an isolated event. CRITICAL maintained; this is an active, multi-domain interstate war requiring renewed third-party mediation, which has already failed twice in 2026.

countries.name: India
incidents: 4 Jul — Uri, Jammu & Kashmir (Line of Control): A Pakistan-administered Kashmir resident who had crossed the LoC on 1 June was handed back to Pakistani authorities via the Kaman Aman Setu crossing — a minor, non-hostile incident.
Ongoing — India-Pakistan border: India continues a demolition drive against unauthorized construction within 0-15km of the international border and tightened anti-infiltration measures.
Late Jun — Beijing: The 35th Working Mechanism for Consultation and Coordination meeting on India-China border affairs continued the post-2025 thaw on delimitation and boundary management.
countries.coa: No significant new security incident this week; both the India-Pakistan ceasefire and India-China LAC de-escalation continue to hold. MODERATE maintained.

countries.name: China
incidents: 23 Jun (context) — Taiwan Strait: The Fujian carrier transited the strait for a third time, described by Beijing as 'routine training' a day after Taipei launched a combat-readiness exercise.

27 Jun — Sea of Japan/East China Sea: China conducted its 11th joint strategic bomber patrol with Russia (2 H-6 plus 2 Tu-95, 2 Tu-142, escorted by J-16s/Su-35), entering South Korea's ADIZ; Seoul scrambled fighters and lodged formal protests, with patrol frequency up from roughly 2/year (2019-23) to 4+/year since 2024.

Late Jun — Beijing: China imposed new export controls on 40 Japanese entities (20 newly listed, 20 moved from watch-list to full control), citing Japan's 'remilitarization' following PM Takaichi's remarks on a potential military response to Chinese aggression against Taiwan; Japan called the move 'completely unacceptable.'

~4 Jul — Scarborough Shoal: Four Chinese warships confronted a Philippine Navy vessel in a rare direct naval standoff, timed to coincide with the conclusion of the roughly three-month, 7,000-troop 'Salaknib 2026' exercise involving the Philippines, US, Japan, New Zealand, and Australia.
countries.coa: China is running a simultaneous four-front grey-zone pressure campaign against Japan (economic coercion), South Korea (joint bomber ADIZ incursion), Taiwan (carrier transits, sustained patrol activity), and the Philippines (direct naval standoff) — no single front is at a kinetic breaking point, but the coordinated timing suggests centrally directed signaling. HIGH maintained; no major military action against Taiwan expected in the 90-day window, but multi-theater intensity is increasing.

countries.name: Taiwan
incidents: 23 Jun — Taiwan Strait: The Fujian carrier's third transit since 23 June was closely monitored by Taiwan's MND; its empty flight deck was assessed as a lower-threat signal.

Jun (month total) — Taiwan-wide: The PLA conducted 134 ADIZ incursions in June, with 12 incursion-free days — continued moderation from the 300+/month peak after May 2024; China Coast Guard conducted repeated patrols near Pratas and Kinmen.
countries.coa: Continuous PLA pressure paired with Taiwan's own readiness posture reflects a stable but tense standoff, with a relative de-escalation in raw incursion numbers since January 2026 even though absolute activity remains historically elevated. HIGH maintained; the mid-July Joint Defense Exercise and August Han Kuang drills are the near-term indicators to watch.

countries.name: Philippines
incidents: ~4 Jul — Scarborough Shoal: Four Chinese warships confronted a Philippine Navy vessel in a rare direct naval standoff, with crews exchanging radio warnings to leave the area; the incident coincided with the conclusion of the 'Salaknib 2026' exercise.

Ongoing — Scarborough Shoal: China Coast Guard ship-days rose from roughly 90/month in H1 2025 to a 2026 average of 156/month, peaking at 216 in May, with China deploying buoys, floating barriers, and a temporary 'research structure' at the shoal.
countries.coa: The 4 July naval standoff, timed the same day US-allied Salaknib drills concluded, is a direct, pointed escalation signal from Beijing and the most significant new Philippines-specific incident of the week. HIGH maintained; continued multilateral patrols and ISR-sharing with Japan/Australia/US, plus diplomatic protest through ASEAN channels, remain the recommended posture.

countries.name: South Korea
incidents: 27 Jun — KADIZ: South Korea scrambled fighters in response to the joint China-Russia bomber patrol entering its air defense identification zone and lodged formal diplomatic protests with both Beijing and Moscow.
24 Jun-early Jul — DMZ: Multiple North Korean soldier crossing/defection incidents were recorded, compounding concern over accelerating North Korean fortification near the Military Demarcation Line.
24 Jun — Yeonpyeong Island: President Lee ordered heightened Marine readiness following North Korean provocation concerns.
countries.coa: Upgraded to HIGH. South Korea is not experiencing domestic incidents directly but is the recipient of compounding North Korean and China-Russia pressure this week — repeated ADIZ scrambles, DMZ fortification proximity, and defector incidents together justify a higher risk posture than the prior baseline. Sustained ADIZ response protocols and diplomatic protest mechanisms are warranted.

countries.name: Vietnam
incidents: Ongoing — Spratly Islands: Vietnamese construction continues at 27 sites across 18+ reefs (roughly 2,771 acres reclaimed cumulatively, including 534 new acres over the past year), with a roughly 13,000-ft runway under construction at Barque Canada Reef.

Ongoing (12-month data) — South China Sea: Chinese vessel transits within 10nm of contested Vietnamese-held features rose to over 100 (45+ coast guard/militia) over the past year, nearly double the prior year's rate.
countries.coa: Structural tension via competing island-building continues to rise, but no acute confrontation (e.g., a fishing-boat ramming incident) occurred this week. MODERATE maintained; satellite monitoring of construction pace on both sides is the key indicator.

countries.name: Japan
incidents: Late Jun — Beijing: China's MOFCOM imposed new export-control sanctions on 40 Japanese entities in direct retaliation tied to the ongoing China-Japan diplomatic crisis stemming from PM Takaichi's November 2025 Taiwan remarks.
27 Jun — Near Japan: The China-Russia joint bomber patrol transited the Sea of Japan/East China Sea/western Pacific, drawing Japan into the ADIZ footprint of the mission.
Ongoing — Senkaku Islands: Japanese fishers continue to be advised to avoid Senkaku waters amid persistent Chinese Coast Guard presence, recorded on 357 of 365 days in 2025.
countries.coa: New economic sanctions represent a concrete escalation atop the unresolved diplomatic crisis and joint military signaling near Japanese waters. MODERATE maintained; assess retaliatory options on the export controls and continue coordinated ADIZ monitoring with South Korea.

countries.name: Indonesia
incidents: 2 Jul — Ipdeheik airstrip, Yahukimo regency, Papua Highlands: West Papua Liberation Army (TPNPB) fighters shot dead American pilot Nicholas Goselin after his aircraft landed, then burned the plane; seven Indigenous Papuan passengers were unharmed — the first confirmed killing of a US citizen in the decades-old conflict.

3 Jul — Yahukimo: Indonesian military recovered and evacuated Goselin's body; TPNPB warned it would target other civilian aircraft it deems supportive of Indonesian military operations and called for UN-facilitated talks.
countries.coa: No major new development beyond body recovery and TPNPB's follow-up threat, which itself raises the risk profile for aviation operating into the Highlands region going forward. HIGH maintained; expect increased US diplomatic engagement and monitor for further TPNPB attacks on civilian/charter aircraft.

countries.name: Thailand
incidents: Ongoing — Thailand-Cambodia border: The ceasefire in effect since 27 December 2025 continues to hold; no new violence was confirmed this week beyond standing diplomatic friction over the stalled Joint Boundary Commission process.
countries.coa: Ceasefire holding but underlying mistrust and a stalled Joint Boundary Commission process leave residual risk. LOW maintained; resumption of JBC talks is the key indicator to monitor.

countries.name: Bangladesh
incidents: Ongoing — Dhaka: No major dated incident this week; continued political competition among the BNP, Jamaat-e-Islami, and the new National Citizen Party following the February 2026 elections remains the standing concern, alongside roughly 1,360 of 3,619 weapons looted from security forces in 2024 still unaccounted for.
countries.coa: A genuinely quiet week. LOW maintained; monitor for renewed inter-party friction and reform implementation.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
incidents: 3 Jul — Colombo: Sri Lanka confirmed deployment of 1,000+ military/police personnel to Haiti's Gang Suppression Force, notable given unresolved allegations of prior misconduct by Sri Lankan peacekeepers in earlier Haiti deployments.
5 Jul — Negombo Prison: A clash between two groups of inmates left 2 dead and dozens injured, triggering separate rooftop protests by male and female prisoners.
countries.coa: These are domestic criminal-justice/prison-security incidents rather than geopolitical developments. LOW maintained; the primary story remains international (Haiti deployment) rather than a domestic security threat.

regions.name: Americas
overview: Venezuela's constitutional clock ran out: the 4 July expiration of interim President Delcy Rodríguez's 180-day mandate, with no National Assembly action to resolve it, opens a legitimacy gap just as the country absorbs an earthquake death toll that has climbed above 3,300.

Colombia's transition to a hardline security posture is now locked in: President-elect De la Espriella's decision to dismantle 'Paz Total' and reactivate arrest warrants from 7 August, combined with continued (if contained) ELN drone/combat operations in Chocó and on the Ecuador border, points to a likely escalation once the handover occurs.

Cuba's protest wave hit a new record in June (107 incidents) and is still spreading — geographically (Santiago near Communist Party headquarters) and demographically (foreign medical students) — while the regime doubles down on repression rather than addressing the underlying fuel-blockade crisis.

Electoral transitions are the connective thread across the Andes: Peru's electoral authority formally certified Keiko Fujimori's razor-thin win on 3 July, with the 15 July credentialing and 28 July inauguration as the next flashpoints, while Ecuador's homicide crisis continues unabated despite an active state of exception.

countries.name: Venezuela
incidents: 3 Jul — Nationwide: The death toll from the 24 June twin earthquakes (7.2/7.5 magnitude, epicenter roughly 160km west of Caracas) reached 2,645 dead and 12,666 injured; by 5 July, the toll rose further to 3,342 dead and 16,740 injured, with 885 buildings damaged (189 collapsed) and 890 aftershocks recorded.

4 Jul — Caracas: Interim President Delcy Rodríguez's 180-day constitutional mandate (begun 5 January 2026 after Maduro's removal) expired with no National Assembly action to declare the presidency vacant or call snap elections, creating an unresolved constitutional gap.

29 Jun (context) — Analysis: Rodríguez is viewed as compromised by both the opposition (as a Maduro-era holdover) and hardliners in her own camp (seen as aligned with the US), with roughly 50,000 people still listed missing on crowdsourced trackers.
countries.coa: Upgraded to CRITICAL. The lapsed constitutional mandate is a genuinely new development that opens the door to legitimacy disputes at precisely the moment the government needs unity to manage earthquake relief on a scale now approaching Haiti-level casualties (3,300+ dead). Oil infrastructure remains undamaged and operational — the one stabilizing factor — but the combined humanitarian and constitutional crisis now outweighs the prior stabilization narrative.

countries.name: Haiti
incidents: Ongoing — Port-au-Prince: At least 26 gangs continue to control roughly 90% of the capital; more than 2,300 killed and 1,100+ injured since the start of 2026, with 1.4 million people displaced nationwide.

Ongoing — Gang Suppression Force: The GSF remains far short of its 5,500-person mandate — only the initial Chadian contingent (400 troops) and a Mongolian force commander are confirmed in place, with full deployment not expected until late 2026; the 14 July UN Security Council briefing remains the key date to watch.

25 Jun (context) — Washington: A US Supreme Court ruling allowing termination of Temporary Protected Status for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the US is expected to generate new migration and remittance pressure on Haiti and the Dominican Republic border in the coming months.
countries.coa: No new mass-casualty gang attack was confirmed this week — a real finding reflecting a grinding, chronic crisis rather than a headline-spiking one — but no improvement either, with the GSF still stalled near roughly 25% of mandated strength. CRITICAL maintained; the 14 July UN Security Council briefing is the key date to watch for any change in international commitment.

countries.name: Colombia
incidents: 3 Jul — San José del Palmar / Chocó-Nariño border: ELN combat operations began, including clashes between the Colombian Army and the ELN's 'Comuneros del Sur' front near the Colombia-Ecuador border; both countries conducted joint patrols in response.

4 Jul — Tadó, Chocó: The ELN carried out two drone-delivered explosive attacks against a police station, marking what the group called a 'bloc anniversary'; no casualties, structural damage only, and Chocó's governor requested funding for counter-drone capability.

4 Jul — National: The ELN publicly criticized the outgoing Petro government for 'wasting the opportunity' for peace talks while warning incoming President-elect De la Espriella it views him with hostility.

5-6 Jul — National: President-elect De la Espriella announced he will dismantle Petro's 'Paz Total' policy from his first decrees, reactivate suspended arrest warrants against armed groups effective 7 August, and gave armed groups 'less than a month' to submit to justice before his inauguration.
countries.coa: The 3-6 July 'armed strike' period materialized as scattered ELN drone/combat actions rather than a nationwide attack wave — a comparatively contained outcome — but De la Espriella's ultimatum sets up a likely confrontation spike once he takes office 7 August and revokes peace-process guarantees. HIGH maintained; expect intensified ELN activity in Arauca/Chocó/Norte de Santander through the transition.

countries.name: Cuba
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Havana (Marianao/Zamora, El Vedado, El Cerro): New cacerolazo protests erupted after residents endured 24+ hours without power and days without water; chants shifted from 'We want power!' to 'Freedom!' and 'Down with the dictatorship!', prompting a heavy police response in El Cerro.

3 Jul — Santiago de Cuba: Cacerolazo protests spread to residential blocks near the Communist Party's provincial headquarters; a Ministry of Interior special-brigade unit arrived carrying long guns to suppress a protest in the Chicharrones neighborhood.
5 Jul — Santiago de Cuba: Foreign medical students at the university's medical faculty joined a cacerolazo over a 24-hour dormitory blackout; power was restored to the building shortly after.
Ongoing — Nationwide: June 2026 recorded 107 protests, a new record and the largest wave since July 2021, as the national electricity deficit hit a record 2,208 MW on 25 June.
countries.coa: Protests are now spreading to new social groups and reaching symbolically sensitive sites near Communist Party headquarters, while the regime's response signals rising repression rather than concessions. CRITICAL maintained; the root cause — the US blockade on Venezuelan oil shipments since December 2025 — remains unresolved, and monitor for potential inflection toward a broader uprising as blackout durations lengthen into summer heat.

countries.name: Ecuador
incidents: 3-5 Jul — Nationwide: At least 15 people were killed in contract-style killings (sicariato) across the country in this three-day span alone.

Ongoing — 10 provinces: The state of exception (declared 16 June for 60 days) remains active across Guayas, Manabí, Santa Elena, Los Ríos, El Oro, Pichincha, Esmeraldas, Santo Domingo de los Tsáchilas, Sucumbíos, and Azuay, suspending home-inviolability protections to allow warrantless raids.

Context — 1 May-12 June: 853 homicides were recorded nationwide, consistent with 2025's record 50.9/100,000 homicide rate, the highest in Ecuador's history and in Latin America for a third consecutive year.
countries.coa: The 15 sicariato killings in just three days confirm the homicide crisis is not abating despite the active state of exception, with Guayas/Guayaquil remaining the epicenter. HIGH maintained; track whether the state of emergency reduces the homicide rate or merely displaces violence.

countries.name: Peru
incidents: 3 Jul — Lima: Peru's National Jury of Elections officially proclaimed Keiko Fujimori president-elect for the 2026-2031 term, confirming her win by just 49,641 votes (9,223,000 to 9,173,000, a 0.27% margin) over Roberto Sánchez.
5 Jul — National: Fujimori began a formal transition-review process following certification of her win; a formal credentials ceremony is scheduled for 15 July ahead of the 28 July inauguration.

Ongoing — Ayacucho and Andean regions: No new mass unrest event was confirmed this week, though Ayacucho (where Fujimori won only 21% support versus 79% for the runner-up) continues to be flagged as the highest-risk region given its history of protests tied to the unresolved 2022-23 protester-death prosecutions.
countries.coa: The formal proclamation is the significant new legal milestone this week — it removes ambiguity about the result but does not resolve the legitimacy dispute given the razor-thin margin and prior fraud allegations. HIGH maintained; watch 15 July and 28 July as the next flashpoints for potential unrest from Ayacucho, Puno, or Juliaca.

countries.name: Bolivia
incidents: 2 Jul — Nationwide: Human Rights Watch published 'Bolivia Cleared the Roads, but it Hasn't Cleared the Crisis,' confirming the seven-week road-blockade crisis ended in late June via an agreement between the Paz administration and the Bolivian Workers' Center, but documenting at least one death during road-clearing operations and seven deaths attributed to blockade-related inability to reach hospitals.

Ongoing — Nationwide: A 90-day state of emergency banning road blockades and mass gatherings remains in effect; HRW cites 22,000 pending Constitutional Court cases and only 12% public trust in the justice system as conditions likely to produce recurring protest cycles.
countries.coa: No new blockades or violence this week — the HRW report is a retrospective structural warning rather than a new incident, confirming that grievances remain unresolved even though roads are clear. MODERATE maintained; press the Paz government toward genuine stakeholder inclusion rather than relying on militarized enforcement.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
incidents: Ongoing — Dajabón: Border trade remains restricted due to Haitian gang activity in the Artibonite zone; security forces maintain heightened vigilance.

25 Jun (context) — Regional: The US Supreme Court's TPS ruling affecting roughly 350,000 Haitians is expected to generate new migration and remittance-loss pressure on the DR border in the coming months, a forward-looking risk rather than a materialized incident.
countries.coa: Genuinely little new security news this week; the main watch item is second-order Haiti/TPS-driven migration pressure building toward the border, not yet materialized as incidents. LOW maintained.

countries.name: Jamaica
incidents: Ongoing — Nationwide: Jamaica Constabulary Force data confirmed serious crimes fell 19.5% in H1 2026 versus H1 2025 (1,464 vs. 1,818 cases); murders down 22.5%, shootings down 27.7%; the JCF projects fewer than 700 murders for the full year versus a historical baseline above 1,000.
countries.coa: Trend continues to improve with no new incidents to report this week, though JCF data flags some parishes bucking the national downward trend. LOW maintained; direct targeted resources to underperforming parishes.
